risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomized 1:1:1 to receive double-blind treatment with oral delayed-release DMF 240 mg BID or TID or placebo [12]. Randomization was centralized and stratified by site." Comment: The study explicitly states that participants were randomized, and the use of centralized, site-stratified randomization is a context clue consistent with a proper random allocation process. Although the exact sequence-generation algorithm is not named, the description is sufficient to judge low risk under a permissive Cochrane approach.

_evidence.method_quote: Patients were randomized 1:1:1 to receive double-blind treatment with oral delayed-release DMF 240 mg BID or TID or placebo [12]. Randomization was centralized and stratified by site.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomized in a 1:1:1 allocation with centralized, site-stratified randomization.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization was centralized and stratified by site." Comment: Central allocation is explicitly described, which prevents the enrolling personnel from foreseeing the next assignment before participant enrolment; this satisfies a low-risk allocation concealment mechanism.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Randomization was centralized and stratified by site.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomized 1:1:1 to receive double-blind treatment with oral delayed-release DMF 240 mg BID or TID or placebo [12]. ... Each study center used separate examining and treating neurologists who were blinded throughout. The examining neurologist conducted neurological assessments while the treating neurologist was responsible for all aspects of patient care, including the treatment of relapses and other disease symptoms." Comment: The study explicitly states double-blind treatment and that both examining and treating neurologists were blinded throughout, indicating participants and key personnel were blinded. Although relapse outcomes could be influenced by knowledge of allocation, the paper describes successful blinding, so the dominant risk for this domain is low.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Patients were randomized 1:1:1 to receive double-blind treatment with oral delayed-release DMF 240 mg BID or TID or placebo [12]. Randomization was centralized and stratified by site. Each study center used separate examining and treating neurologists who were blinded throughout. The examining neurologist conducted neurological assessments while the treating neurologist was responsible for all aspects of patient care, including the treatment of relapses and other disease symptoms.
_evidence.blinding_method: double-blind treatment with oral delayed-release DMF 240 mg BID or TID or placebo; separate examining and treating neurologists who were blinded throughout

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Each study center used separate examining and treating neurologists who were blinded throughout. The examining neurologist conducted neurological assessments while the treating neurologist was responsible for all aspects of patient care... All original digital data for all MRI images were transferred from each of the sites to the MRI reading center for blinded evaluation." Comment: The outcome assessors were explicitly blinded: neurological assessments were done by an examining neurologist who was blinded throughout, and MRI outcomes were centrally read in blinded fashion. The reported outcomes are objective/standardized measures (MRI lesion counts/volumes, PBVC, and neurological assessments handled by a blinded assessor), so lack of blinding is unlikely to have influenced outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote:
Each study center used separate examining and treating neurologists who were blinded throughout. The examining neurologist conducted neurological assessments while the treating neurologist was responsible for all aspects of patient care, including the treatment of relapses and other disease symptoms.

All original digital data for all MRI images were transferred from each of the sites to the MRI reading center for blinded evaluation.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The MRI cohort comprised 540 patients in the ITT population ... and who had any MRI data: 180, 176, and 184 patients in the placebo, delayed-release DMF BID, and delayed-release DMF TID groups, respectively." Comment: For the analysed MRI outcomes, the reported denominators are identical to the numbers randomized to the MRI cohort arms, implying no post-randomization loss on these outcomes in the analysed cohort. The paper also states that the MRI cohort was part of the overall ITT population and that missing MRI data after withdrawal/switching were handled using prespecified imputation methods, which supports a low risk judgement for incomplete outcome data.

per_arm_flow.reasons: The MRI cohort comprised 540 patients in the ITT population who were enrolled at sites that participated in the MRI portion of the study ... and who had any MRI data: 180, 176, and 184 patients in the placebo, delayed-release DMF BID, and delayed-release DMF TID groups, respectively.

The MRI cohort comprised 540 patients in the ITT population ... who had any MRI data: 180, 176, and 184 patients in the placebo, delayed-release DMF BID, and delayed-release DMF TID groups, respectively.

The MRI cohort comprised 540 patients in the ITT population ... who had any MRI data: 180, 176, and 184 patients in the placebo, delayed-release DMF BID, and delayed-release DMF TID groups, respectively.

_evidence.missing_data_method: ITT; MRI cohort subset; imputed data after withdrawal or switching to a rescue medication using a constant rate assumption; for MRI volume data, missing data were imputed using the mean of each treatment group at each visit
_evidence.notes: Numbers reported are for the MRI cohort subset used for the analysed MRI outcomes, not the overall ITT population. The article states the MRI cohort had any MRI data and used ITT as the overall study population; no per-arm attrition counts were provided for the MRI outcome time points.
